title: Revisiting the multi-mode rhombus circuit as a biased-noise qubit
authors: Pablo Aramburu Sanchez, Trevyn F. Q. Larson, Anthony P. McFadden, Constantin Schrade, Joshua Combes, András Gyenis
abstract: In this work, we revisit the idea of using an interferometer of pairs of Josephson junctions as a protected rhombus qubit. Unlike in the original proposal, where the qubit states are encoded into odd and even parity charge states, here, we intentionally alter the energy of one of the junctions to investigate the soft version of the rhombus qubit. This approach allows us to directly probe the qubit transitions over several GHz and reduce the potential drawbacks of the interferometer-based protection. Away from a half flux quantum external field, the large shunting capacitors of the circuit ensure localized qubit states in different phase valleys, leading to a biased-noise qubit. In the realized circuit, we measure an average $T_1\approx500\,μ$s relaxation time in the biased-noise regime (with a Ramsey dephasing time of $T^{R}_\varphi\approx90\,$ns), while an average $T_1\approx27\,μ$s relaxation time at frustration (with $T^{R}_\varphi\approx670\,$ns). Our loss analysis on this multi-mode circuit indicates that at low frequencies, flux noise and quasiparticle tunneling limit the relaxation times, pointing toward the presence of an optimal operating regime of around a few GHz.
summary: The researchers revisit the rhombus qubit by introducing junction asymmetry to create a 'soft' version that functions as a biased-noise qubit. This approach allows for higher transition frequencies that mitigate 1/f flux noise, achieving significantly improved relaxation times. The work identifies an optimal operating regime for these circuits by balancing the trade-offs between different noise channels.
main_problem: The paper addresses the trade-off in rhombus qubits between charge-parity protection and susceptibility to relaxation from flux and quasiparticle noise, specifically seeking to mitigate 1/f flux noise.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a 'soft' rhombus qubit with intentional junction asymmetry can operate as a biased-noise qubit, achieving long relaxation times (T1 approx 500 us) by increasing transition frequency and reducing noise sensitivity.
method: The study combines circuit quantization and numerical simulations of a multi-mode Hamiltonian with experimental characterization using one- and two-tone resonator spectroscopy and Ramsey/echo measurements.
model_or_system: A multi-mode superconducting rhombus circuit consisting of four Josephson junctions in a loop, featuring an asymmetric junction energy (alpha < 1) and large shunting capacitors.
key_observables: Relaxation time (T1), Ramsey dephasing time (Tr), energy-flux dispersion, and resonator transmission.
important_parameters: Junction asymmetry ratio (alpha), external magnetic flux (Phi_ext), charging energy (Ec), Josephson energy (Ej), and quasiparticle density (x_qp approx 10^-8).
main_assumptions: Assumes negligible correlations between tunneling events across different junctions and that the loss contribution of a junction can be neglected near the frustration point.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the protected array concept and wavefunctions; Fig 2 illustrates interference principles and tunneling probability; Fig 3 shows potential energy; Fig 5 displays the device image, energy spectrum, and 3D phase wavefunctions; Fig 6/7/8 show noise contributions, experimental wiring, and circuit schematics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the rhombus qubit concept, proposes the 'soft' asymmetric modification, details the circuit quantization and Hamiltonian modeling, presents experimental fabrication and spectroscopic results, performs a detailed noise analysis (flux, charge, and quasiparticle), and concludes with the identification of an optimal operating regime.
why_interesting: It provides insights into engineering noise-resilient qubits by intentionally breaking symmetries to move into a biased-noise regime, which is highly relevant for open quantum systems and the development of hardware-efficient error correction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The rhombus qubit as the building element for a pro- tected array qubit (a) Schematics of a protected array qubit com- posed of single qubits (green rhombi) coupled through a strong lon- gitudinal interaction (red arrows). Here σz and σx are the Pauli z and x operators, 2Jz is the qubit energy, Jx is the coupling strength. (b), (c) The wavefunctions of the ground and excited states of the charge-parity qubit in phase and charge representations. The inset in b) shows the circuit diagram of the qubit, where the coherent tunnel- ing of only 4e charges is allowed. Arrows indicate the effect of the charge-parity operator ˆP.

FIG. 2. Interference as the principle behind the rhombus (a) Interferometer loop enclosing a magnetic flux Φext and connecting two islands (green dots) through two tunnel barriers (red crosses). A charge can tunnel between the islands through two different paths and pick up phases e±inπ/2 when the loop is biased at frustration. (b) Single-Cooper-pair tunneling probability between the two islands as a function of external flux in the two-junction interferometer. The colors of the curves show the degree of the tunneling symmetry (t1/t2). Complete destructive interference arises when t1 = t2. In the asymmetric case, the suppression of the transmission at Φ0/2 is the remnant of Cooper pair...

FIG. 3. The potential and wavefunctions of the rhombus (a) Theoretical limit, when the qubit phase wavefunctions are approximated by the symmetric and antisymmetric combinations of Dirac deltas located at the minima of the potential at Φext = Φ0/2. Top panel: circuit schematics of the rhombus. Middle panel: a slice of the wavefunctions in charge representation showing a periodic interference-like pattern, where certain charge states are not allowed. Bottom panel: the surface plot of the potential with two minima at (φ1, φ2, φ3) = (±π/4, ±π/4, ±π/4). The dashed lines are guides to the eye for the locations of the potential minima. Fig. 4 shows, this approach has several benefits: (1) the qubit has a higher frequency and experiences less of a 1/f flux noise amplitude (Fig. 4a), (2) the magnitude of the matrix elements of the sin( ˆφi) and sin( ˆφi/2) operators get slightly reduced (Fig. 4b), (3) the charge sensitivity of the device be- comes more suppressed (Fig. 4c), and (4) the curvature of the transition with respect of flux is lowered (Fig. 4d). These ob- servations suggest that the modified version of the rhombus, which we have nicknamed the soft-rhombus qubit, can poten- tially outperform the ideal case of the rhombus, as long as the noise associated with the ˆni operators is not the dominant source of loss at...

FIG. 4. Numerical simulation of the properties of the soft- rhombus qubit (a) The qubit frequency at frustration as a function of the junction asymmetry α. The asymmetry increases the transition frequency, leading to lower 1/f flux noise amplitude at f01. (b) Var- ious matrix elements of the computational states as a function of α. While asymmetry increases the charge matrix element, other matrix elements reduce. (c) The charge dispersion of the qubit transition, which is calculated as the change in frequency when n(1) g is changed by half a period. The qubit becomes less charge-noise sensitive as the asymmetry is increased. (d) The curvature of the qubit frequency vs. flux at frustration...
